Letters from an Observer — June 5, 2026

Today the Department of Energy quietly published the loan guarantee terms for
the next tranche of advanced nuclear projects, and almost no one noticed.

That is a mistake, because the terms tell you where the next decade of American
energy policy is heading. For the first time, the guarantees are explicitly
technology-neutral between fission and the early fusion pilot plants — a
concession that would have been unthinkable even three years ago. The fine
print also ties disbursement to grid-interconnection milestones rather than
construction milestones, which is a direct response to the interconnection
queue backlog that has stranded so much clean capacity.

The political coalition behind this is stranger than it looks. It pairs climate
hawks who want firm, carbon-free power with national-security conservatives who
see Chinese dominance of the reactor supply chain as a strategic threat. When
those two groups agree on an industrial policy, things actually get built.

I have been writing about the slow re-industrialization of the American energy
sector for years, and the through-line is always the same: the bottleneck is
never the technology. It is permitting, transmission, and the willingness to
underwrite first-of-a-kind risk. This DOE notice touches all three.

Watch the interconnection clause. If it survives the inevitable lawsuits, it
will matter more than any single reactor.
